ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling class; no specific beef-and-onion dumpling entry exists. Mapped to the closest broader class FOODON_03315671.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers meat-filled dumplings as the closest defensible category for beef and onion dumplings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for beef and onion dumplings; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef and onion dumplings is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 150 g, commonly consumed as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Beef and Onion Dumplings — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef and Onion Dumplings (cr_id G00010076). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and dietary fibre are not reported in the HPB entry and are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for beef dumplings (USDA SR Legacy #21138 / closest analogue) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on wheat-flour dumplings / pasta-type foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef and onion dumplings. GI estimated at 48 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-flour pasta/dumpling-type foods (GI range 40–55 for boiled wheat-flour products). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 12.05) / 100 ≈ 5.8 for a 150 g serving (12.05 g carbohydrate). Estimation basis: wheat-flour wrapper dominates carbohydrate contribution; beef filling has negligible GI impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium at 46 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Beef and onion dumplings contain only 46 mg sodium per 100 g, well below the 200 mg threshold, making them suitable for hypertensive individuals when consumed without high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chili oil; steamed or boiled preparation is preferred over pan-fried to avoid additional saturated fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (8 g/100 g) from wheat-flour wrapper with low estimated GI (~48)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated GI (~48) and moderate carbohydrate content (8 g/100 g) combined with high protein (8.7 g/100 g) support a moderate post-meal glucose response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to 4–6 pieces and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef filling (~1.4 g/100 g) and cholesterol (28 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The moderate saturated fat content (~1.4 g/100 g) and cholesterol (28 mg/100 g) from the beef filling warrant portion moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; steamed or boiled preparation minimises additional fat, and the low sodium profile (46 mg/100 g) is favourable for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) from red meat amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA are advised to limit consumption and consider replacing beef with chicken, tofu, or fish fillings to reduce pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and onion dumplings (牛肉洋葱饺子) are a classic Chinese dumpling dish consisting of seasoned minced beef and diced onion enclosed in thin wheat-flour wrappers, commonly prepared by steaming, boiling, or pan-frying. The filling typically includes ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil for flavour. The texture is soft and yielding with a savoury, mildly umami filling; pan-fried versions develop a crispy golden base. In Singapore, this dish is widely available at Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and dim sum establishments, served as a main course at lunch or dinner. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 101 kcal, 8.71 g protein, 3.78 g fat, and 8.03 g carbohydrate, with very low sodium (46 mg) and moderate iron (1.79 mg). The high protein content slows gastric emptying and moderates post-meal glucose response, making it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes in controlled portions. The low sodium profile is favourable for hypertension management, though dipping sauces should be used sparingly. The beef filling contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), which may exacerbate inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 48 (low), driven by the wheat-flour wrapper.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef dumplings?
Are beef dumplings high in sodium?
What is the GI of dumplings?
Are beef dumplings suitable for hypertension?
Is beef dumpling good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical entry G00010076, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and dietary fibre estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous wheat-flour dumpling/pasta category with clear documentation.
notes: Saturated fat (1.40 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.55 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.35 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.05 g/100 g), and dietary_fibre_g (0.8 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef dumplings (closest analogue) as these values are not reported in the HPB source entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed vitamin and mineral panel data for this specific preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef which may or may not be halal-certified depending on the preparation establishment. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour pasta/dumpling-type foods from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~48, low category).
